conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first read the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for reaching out. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions.

First, do you have any children under the age of 16, or children under 20 who are in approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I have two children. Alex is 6 and Blake is 4.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. So you have two children - Alex who is 6 years old and Blake who is 4 years old.

Do you currently live in the UK?

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you. Now I need to ask: do both Alex and Blake live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex lives at home with me, but Blake doesn't – Blake lives with my ex-partner.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. 

For Blake, who lives with your ex-partner, do you make any financial contributions towards Blake's upkeep? This could include things like maintenance payments, money for clothes, food, pocket money, or contributions towards their accommodation.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do help out with bits and pieces – I send some money for clothes and things like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. To understand this better, approximately how much do you contribute towards Blake's upkeep each month (or week)? 

Just for reference, the current Child Benefit rate is £25.60 per week for the eldest or only child, and £16.95 per week for additional children.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I send about £20 a week for Blake.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. One more question about Blake - is your ex-partner currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake, or do you know if anyone else is claiming it?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm not sure to be honest. I don't think they are, but I couldn't say for certain.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's okay, thank you for being honest. 

Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment:

Is anyone else (such as a local council) paying towards the accommodation or maintenance costs for either Alex or Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nothing like that. Neither of them are in any sort of care arrangement or anything.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you for clarifying that.

One final question: do you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000 per year? This doesn't affect your eligibility, but it could mean you'd need to pay back some or all of the Child Benefit through a tax charge.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't earn anywhere near that much.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. Based on what you've told me, I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Assessment Complete

**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for both children:**

**For Alex (age 6):** You are eligible because Alex lives with you in the UK and is under 16. This is straightforward - you're responsible for Alex as you live together.

**For Blake (age 4):** You are also eligible because even though Blake lives with your ex-partner, you contribute £20 per week towards Blake's upkeep. Since this exceeds the Child Benefit rate for an additional child (£16.95 per week), you meet the responsibility test for claiming.

**Important points to note:**

- Only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child. Since you're not sure if your ex-partner is claiming for Blake, you should check this. If they are claiming, you and your ex-partner will need to agree between yourselves who should claim. If you can't agree, HMRC will decide.

- If you claim for both children, you would receive £25.60 per week for your eldest child (Alex) and £16.95 per week for your additional child (Blake).

- Since your income is under £60,000, you won't face the High Income Child Benefit tax charge.

You can proceed with making a claim for Child Benefit. If you have any questions or if your circumstances change, you should contact the Child Benefit Office.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 4 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex

circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)

reasonings: Alex is 6 years old (under 16) and lives with you in the UK. You are responsible for Alex as you live together. According to the eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. You'll usually be responsible for a child if you live with them. There are no circumstances that would disqualify this claim (no local council payments, not in care or hospital for extended periods). Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 4 years old (under 16) and lives with your ex-partner, not with you. However, you are contributing £20 per week towards Blake's upkeep (for clothes and other things). According to the eligibility rules, you'll usually be responsible for a child if you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or the equivalent in kind) towards looking after them - for example on food, clothes or pocket money. The Child Benefit rate for an additional child is £16.95 per week. Since you are contributing £20 per week, which exceeds the Child Benefit rate for an additional child, you meet the responsibility test. Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child, so if your ex-partner is not currently claiming (or if they are, you would need to agree who should claim), you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake based on your financial contributions.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You have two children: Alex (6) and Blake (4). You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex who lives with you. You are also eligible for Child Benefit for Blake who lives with your ex-partner, because you contribute £20 per week towards Blake's upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit rate for an additional child (£16.95 per week). However, only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child, so if your ex-partner is also claiming for Blake, you would need to agree between yourselves who should claim, or HMRC will decide. Your income is under £60,000, so you would not face the High Income Child Benefit tax charge.
